content:
Modern **logistic regression** grew from **logit models** in biostatistics — estimating how odds of disease relate to patient covariates — rather than from least-squares curve fitting.

**Pearson** and **Fisher** developed tools for binary outcomes in the early 20th century. **Fisher's** work on **maximum likelihood** gave a principled way to fit models where **y** is discrete but **covariates** are continuous.

**Berkson** (1944) applied the **logistic function** (sigmoid) to transform probabilities into an unbounded scale suitable for linear modeling — the **logit** link was born.

**Cox** and contemporaries extended related ideas to survival analysis; **Nelder** and **Wedderburn** (1972) unified **generalized linear models (GLMs)**, placing logistic regression alongside linear regression as GLMs with different **link** (logit) and **loss** (log-likelihood / BCE).

Computer adoption in the **1970s–90s** made iterative **IRLS** and **L-BFGS** solvers standard; **scikit-learn** and neural-net **binary heads** (linear + sigmoid + BCE) carried the same pairing into modern ML pipelines.

timeline.label: Logit biostatistics (1940s)
timeline.description: Berkson popularizes the logistic curve to model event odds linearly in covariates — origin of logit + sigmoid framing.

timeline.label: GLM framework (1972)
timeline.description: Nelder and Wedderburn formalize logistic regression as a GLM with logit link and Bernoulli log-likelihood (BCE).

timeline.label: Statistical computing (1980s–90s)
timeline.description: Software packages (S, SAS, R, later Python) standardize MLE fitting via IRLS for binary classification.

timeline.label: Deep learning binary heads
timeline.description: Neural networks adopt the same linear layer + sigmoid + BCE pattern at scale — logistic regression as the one-feature-layer limit.
